    16|---
    17|
    18|# Medical Records and Bills Collection
    19|
    20|> Collecting complete medical records and bills from all treatment providers is a prerequisite for demand preparation. This process involves sending HIPAA authorizations, formal record/bill requests via fax and email, and persistent follow-up when providers are slow to respond. Gaps in records collection directly delay case resolution.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|The records collection workflow follows this sequence:
    25|
    26|1. **Provider Identification**: Confirm all treatment providers with the client, including ER, PCP, specialists, imaging
    27|2. **Request Preparation**: Generate HIPAA medical authorization forms and formal records/billing request letters for each provider
    28|3. **Request Delivery**: Send via fax (using RingCentral or similar) and/or email to the provider's records department
    29|4. **Response Tracking**: Track which providers have responded with records vs. bills vs. both
    30|5. **Follow-Up**: If no response within 2-3 weeks, follow up
    31|6. **Client Assistance**: Ask clients to help obtain records from their patient portal (MyChart) when formal requests are delayed
    32|7. **Completeness Check**: Verify all providers are accounted for before moving to demand
    33|
    34|## Evidence
    35|
    36|- In Case B, records/billing requests were sent to St. Elizabeth Florence Hospital via email to a specific contact (Dawn.Probst@stelizabeth.com). The billing info was returned same day. However, getting complete records from Christ Hospital Physicians Primary Care took significantly longer.
    37|- In Case C, records were requested from Baptist Health Louisville via fax on 4/29/2024. Records were received by 5/13/2024 (14 days). A task to "review records and add to Timeline" was created once received.
    38|- In Case B, the client's spouse provided medical visit details from the patient portal (MyChart) while waiting for formal records, which helped move the case forward.
    39|- In Case C, the attorney flagged that records collection had stalled with "no action" for a month, emphasizing that negotiation cannot proceed without complete records.
    40|- In Case D (VA patient), records requests to VA Medical Center Lexington took over 12 months to yield results. Initial request mailed Nov 2023; VA OGC responded Jul 2024. Even after receiving records, they were incomplete — the client reported additional MRIs, physical therapy (VA IPRP program), and injections not in the file. The firm had to ask the client to review records and identify what was missing. The attorney noted: "We're missing a lot of stuff, and that's just part and parcel to the VA."
    41|- In Case D (premises liability, litigation), records had to be requested as CERTIFIED copies for use in litigation — standard copies were insufficient. The certification required correction (date range "04/2014 thru Current"). Records were delivered via Box.com file sharing from ROI quality analysts (e.g., Medicopy). Multiple providers were involved: Grace Health (PCP), Norton/Khalily (orthopedics), CHI St. Joseph (orthopedics + radiology), Baptist Health Corbin, and Corbin Imaging. Records were re-requested to get updated records during active litigation.
    42|- In Case E (auto accident), records review during demand preparation revealed referrals to providers not previously on file (White House Clinics, King's Eye Care). These had to be added and their records requested, delaying demand preparation. Records from one provider (H2 Health) were requested by calling, faxing, and re-faxing over weeks.
    43|- In Case D (20+ providers across 4 states), initial records/bills requests were sent in Feb-Apr 2023, but many providers had not responded by mid-2024, requiring a complete second round of "Certified Medical Records & Billing Request" letters in Aug 2024 — nearly 18 months after the first round. Multiple channels were used simultaneously (email, fax via RingCentral, ChartSwap, EpicLink). Provider confusion arose when billing names didn't match clinical names (e.g., "Norton Leatherman" vs "Norton Neuro & Spine" returned "No Records" despite bills existing).
    44|- In Case F (WC case), records were requested from Medical Center of Scottsville and Riverside Orthopedics via fax within the first week of file setup. The Med Center required a DocuSign-verified authorization (the standard fax copy was rejected), adding a round-trip delay. The provider also needed clarification on scope ("only ER records or all records"). Records arrived piecemeal — radiology CD first, then billing statements separately. A third-party billing entity (TeamHealth) sent bills that were associated with the hospital visit but under a different entity name. Multiple new providers were added over 2+ years as treatment progressed (pain management, shoulder injections, MRI facilities).
    45|- In Case H (MVA, air evac), records collection involved an air ambulance service (Air Evac EMS) requiring fax to a distant location. ChartSwap was used for TriStar Skyline Medical Center records retrieval. The case involved records from providers across multiple states (TN hospital where air-lifted, KY local providers). The air evacuation bill alone was $72K+, making complete billing documentation critical.
    46|- In Case G (MVA, companion cases), records collection was complicated by a companion case (daughter) requiring separate hospital records from Children's Hospital. The client was asked to obtain companion records but failed to do so over months (Oct 2024 to Feb 2025+). Chiropractic records from Southgate Chiropractic were also needed.
    47|- In Case I (MVA), records were requested from UofL Health St. Mary & Elizabeth Hospital and chiropractic provider via fax within the first week. Provider sent records via ChartSwap platform. The PIP adjuster proactively asked about medical records even before the firm's formal requests were complete.
    48|- In a PL case (retail slip-and-fall, 10+ providers), records collection extended over 2+ years. Even during active litigation (July 2025), the firm was still making outbound calls to providers (orthopedics, therapy, PCP) for outstanding records and bills. The medical authorization form was rejected by one provider because it was missing the date of the patient's signature and their DOB — requiring client callback and resubmission, confirming that incomplete authorization forms are a recurring records collection bottleneck. ChartSwap was used as an intermediary for some provider records.
    49|- In an MVA case with companion plaintiffs, certified medical records were requested from a university hospital and chiropractic provider for litigation. A billing error was discovered in the chiropractic records (incorrect certified billing amount) requiring correction correspondence. This confirms that certified records must be audited — providers can certify incorrect billing amounts. Bills from EMS providers were also collected for PIP submission. The case used EvenUP for case valuation, requiring complete records before the evaluation could be generated.
    50|- In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff, DOI 8/2023), records were requested from 6 providers including EMS, a university hospital system (separate hospital and physician entities), chiropractic, pharmacy, and pain management. The hospital system had distinct billing entities — hospital bills were requested separately from physician bills, and each was received on different dates (spanning Nov-Dec 2024). The PIP claim was set up same day and PIP info provided to the hospital immediately. The firm also requested and received the police report from the out-of-state police department (New Albany, IN) — the report was not immediately available the day after the accident and required follow-up.
    51|- In Case N (premises liability estate, DOI 7/2020), records collection was complicated by provider misidentification. The firm initially requested records from "CareTenders" (a home health agency), only to discover no records existed — the actual provider was a different home health company. The surviving spouse later provided a calendar from the correct provider, enabling the correct request. Records from one diagnostic imaging provider were requested via ChartSwap, while records from the hospital required a separate LOR to the legal department before records could be released. Bills for the home health provider remained outstanding even 3+ years later during active litigation — the attorney noted during deposition prep that bills were missing. This confirms that provider misidentification is a persistent risk, especially for home health and similar ancillary providers where patients may not know the exact entity name.
    52|
    53|## Agent Guidance
    54|
    55|1. Create a provider checklist during file setup with records requested/received dates for each
    56|2. Send HIPAA authorizations and record requests within the first week of file setup
    57|3. Use both fax and email when possible — some providers respond faster to one channel
    58|4. Set follow-up tasks at 14-day intervals for each outstanding request
    59|5. Encourage clients to share records from patient portals as an interim measure
    60|6. Track records and bills separately — receiving one does not mean you have the other
    61|7. Do not move to demand preparation until ALL providers have been accounted for
    62|8. When the attorney asks about case status, have the records collection status ready
    63|
    64|## Pitfalls
    65|
    66|- Not confirming all providers with the client early (discovering a provider late delays everything)
    67|- Sending requests and not tracking responses
    68|- Not distinguishing between records received and bills received (you need both)
    69|- Letting the client's medical portal records substitute for formal provider records in the demand
    70|- Waiting too long to follow up on non-responsive providers
    71|- Requesting records under a billing entity name that differs from the clinical entity name (causes "no records found" responses)
    72|- Not anticipating that multi-state providers require different request methods and response timelines
    73|
    74|## Related
    75|
    76|- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP needs bills to process; records collection feeds PIP workflow
    77|- [[demand-timing-strategy]] — demand cannot be sent until records are complete
    78|- [[provider-communication-patterns]] — how different providers respond to requests
    79|- [[government-entity-records-collection]] — VA and other government providers require distinct handling with much longer timelines
    80|- [[premises-liability-case-patterns]] — PL cases in litigation require certified records
    81|- [[demand-preparation-workflow]] — records completion gates the demand phase
    82|", "total_lines": 81, "file_size": 10929, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

     7|confidence: medium

     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|related:
    12|  - client-frustration-escalation

    16|---
    17|
    18|# Unreachable Client Patterns
    19|
    20|> Some PI clients are persistently difficult to reach — their voicemail is full, they don't return calls, and emails go unanswered. This pattern is distinct from client frustration (where the client is actively trying to reach the firm). Unreachable clients create a bottleneck because the firm needs their input to proceed with intake, treatment confirmation, and case management. Persistent multi-channel contact attempts are required, sometimes over weeks.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|### Common Unreachability Signals
    25|1. **Voicemail full**: The client's voicemail box is full, preventing the firm from leaving a message
    26|2. **No answer, no callback**: Multiple outbound calls with no answer and no return call
    27|3. **Phone number changes**: Client changes phone numbers without notifying the firm
    28|4. **Email unresponsive**: Welcome emails and information requests go unanswered
    29|
    30|### Multi-Channel Escalation
    31|The firm follows a predictable escalation pattern when a client is unreachable:
    32|1. **Day 1**: Outbound call → no answer / voicemail full
    33|2. **Day 1**: Text message sent as fallback
    34|3. **Day 2-3**: Second outbound call attempt + email
    35|4. **Week 2**: Follow-up task created ("Two Week follow up with client")
    36|5. **Week 3-4**: Additional call attempts, emails, texts
    37|6. **Ongoing**: "Still unreachable" logged in case notes
    38|7. **Eventually**: Client responds via a different channel or new phone number
    39|
    40|### Impact on Case Timeline
    41|- Intake tasks cannot be completed without client input (providers list, insurance card, SSN)
    42|- File Setup stalls — the case sits in File Setup for months instead of progressing
    43|- Medical records requests are delayed because providers aren't confirmed
    44|- The case may ultimately be declined partly due to the difficulty of working it without client cooperation
    45|
    46|### Client Eventually Resurfaces
    47|Unreachable clients often do eventually make contact, sometimes with a new phone number. When they do:
    48|- They expect the case to be progressing despite their absence
    49|- They may provide the requested information belatedly
    50|- The firm must quickly re-engage and catch up on delayed tasks
    51|
    52|## Evidence
    53|
    54|- In an auto accident case (DOI 3/2024), the client was unreachable from day one of file setup. Multiple calls were made with the note "No answer, vm is full." Text messages were sent as fallback. The client eventually responded via text ("I will call you later, thanks") and then called back from a different number. The voicemail transcription showed they had also been trying to reach the firm: "We have missed each other for the last day or 2." Despite repeated outreach attempts over weeks, the client remained difficult to reach throughout the case. By 2/2025, the note still read "Still unreachable." The client's phone number changed at least once during the case.
    55|- In the same case, even when the firm was trying to close/decline the case, a staff member noted they needed to speak with the client about the case update — and had to use a different phone number than the one on file.
    56|- In a PL case (DOI 9/2023) in active litigation, the client called the attorney repeatedly (20+ missed calls over 7 months), but the calls went unreturned for extended periods. The attorney then made periodic outbound calls. This is a reverse unreachability pattern — the firm is unreachable to the client rather than vice versa, likely due to attorney bandwidth in a litigation-heavy caseload.
    57|- In an elderly MVA case (DOI 1/2022) in litigation, the client's voicemail was explicitly noted as "full" when the firm attempted to call with case updates. The client called back repeatedly but calls were not answered. This voicemail-full pattern prevents both parties from leaving messages, creating a complete communication breakdown.
    58|- In an auto accident case (DOI 4/2023, KY) in active litigation, the firm attempted to call the client multiple times but the phone "just rings and then gives busy signal." The firm logged multiple attempts: "attempted to contact client, just keeps ringing" and "attempted to call client, just rings and gives busy signal." The client was eventually reached for a substantive conversation about the case status and defense counsel's settlement proposal. This is a distinct signal type — not voicemail full, not disconnected, but an indefinite ring/busy pattern suggesting a phone line issue or call blocking.
    59|- In an auto accident case (DOI 1/2024, KY) during a scheduled Zoom deposition, the firm had to text the client urgently: "Dana you good? We lost you" and "They were having some technical problems. Go ahead and log back on." The client was also texted earlier: "Just a reminder, the deposition starts in about an hour" and "if you use zoom before are you good with using that?" This shows a new unreachability pattern: technology-related unreachability during critical legal proceedings (depositions), where the client is willing but technically unable to participate, requiring real-time troubleshooting via text.
    60|- In a trucking MVA case (DOI 2/2024), the client's phone number was "not in service" when the firm tried to reach her during intake. A text was sent and a second number was tried. The client was eventually reached via an alternate number. This was a pattern of phone service disruption rather than willful non-response.
    61|- In an MVA case (DOI 1/2023), the client called the firm repeatedly but no one answered — calls were logged as "missed" to multiple staff members. The client also texted: "Is your office open today?" The firm's unreachability to the client (many missed inbound calls over months) is the reverse pattern, creating friction and frustration.
    62|- In an MVA case (DOI 1/2023), the client was initially unreachable — "call went to VM - left msg." The client then used an alternate phone number for subsequent contact. New phone numbers should always be captured immediately.
    63|- In a treatment-phase MVA case (DOI 8/2024), the client preferred text over phone calls. When staff called, the client was unavailable ("on an errand"). Text became the primary channel. Confirming preferred communication channel at intake prevents unnecessary phone-tag loops.
    64|- In a demand-phase case (DOI 12/2025), the client's voicemail was full when the firm tried to call with a case update. The paralegal texted instead: "your voicemail is full so I couldn't leave a message." Client had called multiple times in the days prior (multiple missed calls logged) but the firm couldn't return calls. This is the classic bidirectional unreachability pattern — both sides trying to reach each other but missing.
    65|- In a file-setup MVA case (DOI 8/2025), the client called multiple times (missed calls logged weeks apart) but received no callbacks. The case was in demand preparation but the client had no updates. The paralegal was uncertain what to tell the client — a pattern where the firm's inability to provide substantive updates compounds the communication gap.
    66|- In an MVA case (DOI 9/2025) with disputed liability, both insurance carriers were denying fault. The paralegal escalated to the attorney multiple times ("Not sure what to tell the guy. Any ideas?") but received no response documented in the logs. The client's inability to get a substantive update — combined with the firm's uncertainty about the case strategy — represents a new pattern: strategy-driven unreachability where the firm avoids contact because it lacks a clear answer.
    67|- In a file-setup MVA case (DOI 9/2024, KY), the initial contact attempt to the client went to voicemail which was full. The paralegal noted "Went straight to vm. VM is full." The client's boyfriend (a companion/passenger in the same accident) had initially called in. Multiple attempts were needed before the client could be reached for a welcome call. The pattern of VM-full on day one of file setup was confirmed in 3 additional cases.
    68|- In an MVA case (DOI mid-2025, KY), the client called the firm's main line (missed calls were logged to the attorney) but the firm did not return calls promptly. The missed call logs showed repeated attempts weeks apart. This reverse unreachability pattern — where the client is available but the firm is not — was confirmed across 4+ cases in this batch, particularly with smaller or early-stage cases that may not receive priority callback attention.
    69|- In a case (DOI mid-2025, KY), the client was described as "difficult" and repeatedly asked to meet with the attorney in person. The paralegal communicated to the attorney: "[Client] is difficult. Nice guy but keeps asking to meet with you." This is a distinct communication pattern — not unreachability per se, but a client whose communication needs exceed the standard phone/text model, requiring in-person attorney engagement.
    70|
    71|## Agent Guidance
    72|
    73|1. If the first call goes to voicemail and VM is full, immediately send a text message
    74|2. If no response to text, send an email the same day
    75|3. Set a 2-week follow-up task automatically on every case where the client has not responded
    76|4. Log EVERY contact attempt (call, text, email) with outcome (answered, VM full, no answer, etc.)
    77|5. After 3 failed contact attempts, escalate to the attorney with a note about unreachability
    78|6. Always ask the client for a secondary contact number and an emergency contact
    79|7. When the client does respond, immediately gather all needed information in that same conversation — you may not get another chance soon
    80|8. Record the client's updated phone number in the case system immediately when it changes
    81|9. Consider whether unreachability is a pattern that makes the case unworkable — flag for attorney review
    82|
    83|## Pitfalls
    84|
    85|- Only trying to call and not using text/email as fallback
    86|- Not logging failed contact attempts (creates the appearance of no effort)
    87|- Waiting weeks between contact attempts instead of escalating channels
    88|- Not asking for a secondary contact number at intake
    89|- Not updating the phone number in the system when the client calls from a new number
    90|- Assuming unreachability means the client is uninterested — they may be dealing with medical/life issues
    91|
    92|## Related
    93|
    94|- [[client-frustration-escalation]] — opposite pattern where the client is trying to reach the firm
    95|- [[client-expectations-management]] — unreachable clients may still have expectations about case progress
    96|- [[intake-and-onboarding-workflow]] — unreachability most critically impacts intake tasks
    97|- [[case-decline-and-closing-workflow]] — persistent unreachability may contribute to case decline decisions
    98|", "total_lines": 97, "file_size": 11056, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    16|---
    17|
    18|# Police Records and Open Records Requests
    19|
    20|> Obtaining police records (crash reports, body camera footage, dispatch logs, RTCC footage) requires Open Records Requests (ORR) to the relevant law enforcement agency. Unlike medical records requests, ORRs go through government public records processes with distinct timelines, portals, and follow-up requirements. Multiple ORRs may be needed for different evidence types, and processing times vary from days to months.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|### Types of Police Records
    25|1. **Crash/Incident Report**: The basic police report documenting the accident
    26|2. **Body Camera Footage**: Officer body-cam video from the scene, delivered via Evidence.com (Axon platform)
    27|3. **Dash Camera Footage**: In-car video from patrol vehicles
    28|4. **RTCC Footage**: Real-Time Crime Center camera footage if available in the area
    29|5. **Dispatch/CAD Logs**: Computer-aided dispatch records
    30|6. **Related Reports**: If the accident involved a stolen vehicle or other criminal activity, separate reports exist
    31|
    32|### ORR Process — Louisville Metro (LMPD)
    33|1. Submit ORR via Louisville Metro Government public records portal (NextRequest platform)
    34|2. Receive submission confirmation with request number (e.g., "24-15008")
    35|3. Wait for processing — timeline varies dramatically (days to months)
    36|4. May receive partial fulfillment (e.g., one officer's body cam but not another's)
    37|5. Submit additional ORRs for remaining evidence
    38|6. Download evidence via secure links (Axon Evidence.com for body cam)
    39|
    40|### ORR Process — Smaller Departments
    41|- Smaller police departments (e.g., Bowling Green PD) may process ORRs by phone
    42|- May require follow-up calls to check status
    43|- Contact information provided during the request may become stale over time
    44|- Department may call back months later asking if the request is still needed
    45|
    46|### Multiple ORRs for One Case
    47|A single case may require 3-5+ separate ORRs:
    48|- 1st ORR: Initial crash report and primary officer body cam
    49|- 2nd ORR: Additional officer body cam footage
    50|- 3rd ORR: RTCC footage
    51|- 4th ORR: Footage from related incidents (e.g., theft report body cam)
    52|- Each ORR is tracked with a separate request number
    53|
    54|### Body Cam Review
    55|When body cam footage is received:
    56|- Download from the Axon Evidence.com secure link (link expires)
    57|- Review footage and create a summary noting key observations
    58|- Forward relevant footage to defense counsel in litigation
    59|- Note client/witness behavior, officer observations, and scene conditions
    60|
    61|## Evidence
    62|
    63|- In a litigation case (DOI 1/2021), at least 4 separate ORRs were submitted to LMPD over several months. The 1st ORR yielded the crash report and one officer's body cam. Review of the body cam revealed key details about the scene and driver behavior. A 2nd ORR was needed for the second officer's body cam, RTCC footage, and a related theft report's body/dash cam. Each ORR was tracked via the Louisville Metro NextRequest portal. Evidence was downloaded via Axon Evidence.com links and forwarded to defense counsel.
    64|- In another case (DOI 3/2024), an ORR to Bowling Green PD was submitted but took months to process. The PD called back asking for updated contact information — the original contact info had become stale. Even after the case was being closed, the ORR was still being processed, with the PD "finally finishing up" the request months later.
    65|
    66|## Agent Guidance
    67|
    68|1. Submit ORRs during file setup — do not wait until litigation
    69|2. Track each ORR with its request number and submitting portal
    70|3. For LMPD: use the Louisville Metro NextRequest portal; for other departments, check if they have an online portal or require email/fax
    71|4. Set follow-up reminders: check ORR status every 2 weeks for the first month, then monthly
    72|5. When partial evidence is received, immediately identify what's still missing and submit a follow-up ORR
    73|6. Download body cam footage immediately when the Axon link is provided — links may expire
    74|7. Create a written summary of body cam footage highlighting key observations
    75|8. In litigation, forward all evidence to defense counsel promptly
    76|9. Even on closed/declined cases, complete pending ORRs — the records may be needed
    77|
    78|## Pitfalls
    79|
    80|- Submitting a single ORR and assuming all evidence will be included — multiple ORRs are usually needed
    81|- Not tracking ORR request numbers and follow-up dates
    82|- Letting Axon Evidence.com download links expire
    83|- Not providing updated contact information to the police department
    84|- Assuming small-town PDs will process ORRs as quickly as larger departments
    85|- Not reviewing body cam footage for case-relevant details before forwarding
    86|
    87|## Related
    88|
    89|- [[government-entity-records-collection]] — ORRs are a form of government records collection with similar delays
    90|- [[medical-records-collection]] — parallel records collection process for medical providers
    91|- [[litigation-process-patterns]] — police records become critical evidence in litigation
    92|- [[case-decline-and-closing-workflow]] — ORRs may still be pending when a case is declined
    93|", "total_lines": 92, "file_size": 5521, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

   135|- **Litigation requires certified records**: Standard copies of medical records are insufficient for court use; must be re-requested as certified if not done initially

   292|### Key New Patterns Identified
   293|- **Workers' compensation cases require a different workflow**: WC claims involve adjuster approval for treatment, TTD payments, AWW disputes, and DWC proceedings — all absent from auto accident PI cases. The PHASE_DAG may need WC-specific milestones.

   297|- **Case manager handoffs create knowledge gaps**: When cases outlast their original case manager, the incoming staff may re-request known information, damaging client trust.
   298|- **SOL alerts run on closed cases**: Filevine's automated SOL countdown does not account for case status — alerts continue even after a case is declined.
   299|- **4+ year litigation cases exist**: Multi-defendant cases with uninsured drivers can extend far beyond the typical 2-3 year PI case lifecycle.

   327|2. `client-expectations-management` — evidence_count: 3->6, confidence: low->medium

   331|6. `case-phase-transitions` — evidence_count: 5->8, confidence: low->medium (dental bypass demand, UIM parallel track, coverage-denial forced litigation)
   332|7. `attorney-review-triggers` — evidence_count: 5->8 (coverage denial challenge, UIM complaint direction, dental records management)

   334|9. `pre-settlement-funding-obligations` — evidence_count: 2->4 ($31K funding in UIM case)

   338|### Confidence Upgrades
   339|- `client-frustration-escalation`: low -> medium (5 cases)
   340|- `client-expectations-management`: low -> medium (6 cases)
   341|- `statute-of-limitations-monitoring`: low -> medium (5 cases)
   342|- `lien-management-health-insurance`: low -> medium (5 cases)
   343|- `pip-claims-management`: low -> medium (5 cases)
   344|- `case-phase-transitions`: low -> medium (8 cases)
   345|
   346|### Key New Patterns Identified
   347|- **UIM claims require a separate litigation track**: When BI limits are insufficient, UIM complaints are filed against the client's own insurer in a separate court, with separate discovery and defense counsel
   348|- **Coverage denial creates the longest case timelines**: When a BI carrier denies coverage (stolen vehicle), the case can extend 4+ years with coverage investigation, EUOs, and forced litigation
   349|- **Kentucky Assigned Claims (KAC) is the fallback PIP mechanism**: When no BI coverage exists, KAC assigns a carrier for PIP benefits — but the process involves negotiation about which bills are covered
   350|- **Medicaid liens (Conduent) are actively monitored**: Unlike passive private insurer liens, Conduent calls the firm to check on case status — and Medicaid liens can be the largest single deduction
   351|- **Dental injuries create unique timeline pressure**: Staged implant procedures (18+ months) push cases toward the SOL, often requiring litigation filing before treatment is complete
   352|- **Same carrier for multiple claim types creates confusion**: When PIP, BI, or UIM are at the same carrier, different adjusters handle each track — but the firm must carefully distinguish which claim it's communicating about
   353|- **Pre-settlement funding of $31K+ can exceed net recovery**: In complex cases with large liens and extensive treatment, pre-settlement funding can consume most or all of the client's share
   354|
   355|### Gaps Partially Addressed
   356|- **UIM claim processing**: Now documented from one case; need more to understand negotiation/settlement patterns
   357|- **Medicaid/Conduent liens**: Now documented from two cases; need more to understand negotiation/reduction possibilities
   358|- **Litigation workflow**: Three more cases added (now 4 total); confidence increasing
   359|

   361|- Settlement disbursement: Still no cases reached completed disbursement
   362|- Counter-offer exchange documentation: Detailed back-and-forth negotiation tactics still undocumented
   363|- UIM settlement patterns: Need examples of UIM settlement amounts and policy limit interactions
   364|- Coverage denial outcomes: Need to see how coverage denial cases ultimately resolve

   413|### Key New Patterns Identified
   414|- **Dog bite cases follow a distinct liability investigation pattern**: Unlike auto accidents, the firm must identify the animal owner, property owner, and homeowner's insurance through address searches and certified mail — a process that can take months
   415|- **SOL pressure on transfer cases creates emergency filing**: When a case is transferred from prior counsel with SOL approaching (<60 days), the firm may need to onboard and file a complaint on the same day — skipping demand entirely
   416|- **Client death during proceedings is a real edge case**: A WC client died before their deposition, requiring immediate procedural pivots and family notification handling
   417|- **Bidirectional unreachability exists**: In some cases, both the client AND the firm are unreachable to each other (client calls go unanswered, firm's return calls go to voicemail full) — creating complete communication breakdown
   418|- **Guardian communication in minor's cases follows parent-proxy patterns**: In dog bite cases with minor clients, the guardian handles all communication, court appearances, and deposition scheduling
   419|- **WC monitoring posture is a valid strategy**: Some WC cases are monitored passively (no LOR sent, no active participation) while the client receives TTD and treatment through the WC carrier, with the firm only engaging when litigation begins
   420|- **EvenUP is used as a case evaluation tool**: In at least one case, EvenUP expenses indicate it was used for AI-assisted case valuation during litigation

   458|### Key New Patterns Identified
   459|- **Wrongful death cases fundamentally alter the case lifecycle**: No treatment, no records collection, no demand — the case goes directly from intake to investigation and litigation. The PHASE_DAG must account for this exception.
   460|- **Third-party claims administrators add complexity to PL cases**: Large retailers use third-party claims management (not their own insurer), creating an additional layer of communication.
   461|- **WC estate claims face immediate procedural challenges**: Claims filed in a deceased individual's name are denied on sight — the Motion to Substitute Plaintiff is a critical first step.
   462|- **WC cases can take 5+ years**: The estate WC case (DOI 12/2020, Board opinion 2/2025) is the longest observed case timeline, confirming that WC administrative proceedings are slower than even complex PI litigation.
   463|- **Multi-plaintiff cross-state filings add jurisdictional complexity**: Filing an MVA case in Indiana when the firm is KY-based requires coordination across state court systems.
   464|- **Certified records billing errors occur**: Providers can certify incorrect billing amounts, requiring audit and correction correspondence before records can be used in litigation.
   465|- **Authorization form rejections are a recurring bottleneck**: Missing signature dates, missing DOB on authorization forms causes provider rejection and adds round-trip delay.
   466|- **Settlement release negotiations in wrongful death cases span months**: Multiple defense counsel, Medicare considerations, and large liens ($171K+ WC lien) create extended resolution timelines.

    17|
    18|# Insurance Coverage Gaps Drive Case Decline
    19|
    20|> This connection documents how [[uninsured-motorist-pip-only-cases]] directly causes [[case-decline-and-closing-workflow]] when coverage investigation reveals no viable recovery path. The interaction between [[pip-claims-management]] and [[intake-and-onboarding-workflow]] determines how quickly this gap is identified — early identification prevents wasted resources, while late discovery wastes months of effort.
    21|
    22|## Connection
    23|
    24|Three concepts form a decision chain:
    25|
    26|1. **[[intake-and-onboarding-workflow]]** should include early coverage verification (BI status of at-fault driver, UM status of client's policy)
    27|2. **[[uninsured-motorist-pip-only-cases]]** arises when both BI and UM are absent, leaving only PIP through KAC
    28|3. **[[case-decline-and-closing-workflow]]** is triggered when the attorney determines the case is not viable with PIP-only recovery
    29|
    30|### The Time Cost of Late Discovery
    31|When coverage gaps are not identified early:
    32|- The firm may spend 3-6+ months on intake, records requests, and provider management before discovering the gap
    33|- PIP payments may have already been made or requested
    34|- The client relationship is established, making decline more difficult
    35|- Staff time is wasted on a case with no viable outcome
    36|
    37|### The KAC Parallel
    38|KAC involvement (Kentucky Assigned Claims Plan) is often the first signal of a coverage gap. If KAC is the only path to PIP, it means the client may have no auto insurance — which usually means no UM coverage either.
    39|
    40|## Evidence
    41|
    42|- In one case, the coverage gap (no BI, no UM, PIP-only through KAC) was not flagged until months after intake. The case manager noted "No BI or UM only PIP through KAC" and requested permission to decline. The attorney authorized the decline, but significant resources (records requests, Air Evac provider outreach, repeated client contact attempts) had already been spent.
    43|- In another case, KAC was involved from the beginning for PIP, but the BI claim existed through a named defendant's carrier. The case proceeded to litigation rather than decline.
    44|
    45|## Agent Guidance
    46|
    47|1. Check BI and UM/UIM status within the first week of file setup — before investing in records requests
    48|2. If KAC is involved for PIP, this is a yellow flag — immediately verify whether BI and/or UM exist
    49|3. When the answer is "PIP-only," flag for attorney review within 48 hours
    50|4. Do not initiate expensive tasks (Air Evac records, specialist records) until coverage viability is confirmed
    51|
    52|## Related
    53|
    54|- [[uninsured-motorist-pip-only-cases]] — the coverage gap pattern
    55|- [[case-decline-and-closing-workflow]] — the decline process triggered by the gap
    56|- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP is the only benefit in these cases
    57|- [[intake-and-onboarding-workflow]] — early coverage verification prevents wasted effort
    58|", "total_lines": 57, "file_size": 3292, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    16|
    17|# PIP Processing Delays Cause Collection Anxiety
    18|
    19|> This connection documents how delays in [[pip-claims-management]] directly cause [[medical-bill-collections-anxiety]], which in turn creates pressure on [[client-expectations-management]] and can push clients toward premature settlement.
    20|
    21|## Connection
    22|
    23|Three concepts form a causal chain:
    24|
    25|1. **PIP Processing Delays** ([[pip-claims-management]]): Bills submitted to PIP may sit unprocessed for 30-60+ days. In one observed case, NO payments were made after 60 days.
    26|
    27|2. **Bills Go to Collections** ([[medical-bill-collections-anxiety]]): Medical providers typically send bills to collections after 60-90 days of non-payment. If PIP hasn't paid, the bill goes to collections while the firm is still managing the case.
    28|
    29|3. **Client Pressure** ([[client-expectations-management]]): Clients receive collection notices and become anxious about their credit. They contact the firm with increasing urgency, eventually expressing desire to "put this to bed" regardless of case value.
    30|
    31|This chain creates a situation where administrative delays in PIP processing directly undermine the firm's negotiating position by creating client urgency to settle.
    32|
    33|## Evidence
    34|
    35|- In Case C, bills were submitted to State Farm PIP on 11/1/2024 but no payments were made as of 12/31/2024. Meanwhile, the client received collection notices from Credit Bureau Systems and came into the office concerned about his credit.
    36|- In Case B, the client's husband forwarded collection notices in November 2025 and expressed urgency: "I don't want this to hurt our credit" and "We want this to go away." This coincided with outstanding PIP processing.
    37|
    38|## Insight
    39|
    40|Aggressive PIP follow-up is one of the highest-leverage activities a paralegal can perform. Reducing PIP processing time from 60+ days to 30 days would eliminate most collection anxiety, which in turn would reduce client pressure for premature settlement.
    41|
    42|## Prevention Protocol
    43|
    44|1. Submit bills to PIP within 48 hours of receipt
    45|2. Set a 14-day follow-up task (not 30 days) for each PIP submission
    46|3. If no confirmation of processing at 14 days, call the PIP adjuster
    47|4. Proactively inform clients: "We have submitted your bill to your auto insurance for payment. This typically takes 2-4 weeks."
    48|5. If a collection notice arrives, respond within 24 hours with status and timeline
    49|
    50|## Related
    51|
    52|- [[pip-claims-management]] — the root cause of the delay
    53|- [[medical-bill-collections-anxiety]] — the symptom experienced by clients
    54|- [[client-expectations-management]] — proactive communication prevents escalation
    55|", "total_lines": 54, "file_size": 3017, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

   805|### Key New Patterns Identified
   806|- **Client at fault as decline trigger**: When the police report shows the firm's client was at fault, the case is declined regardless of injuries or treatment. This is a distinct trigger from coverage gaps — "CLIENT AT FAULT" noted in file, paralegal asks attorney "ok to decline and close?"
   807|- **Incarcerated clients during negotiation**: A client discovered to be in county jail during active negotiation creates settlement authorization barriers. The firm continued case work (PIP ledger requests, adjuster correspondence) but could not obtain client input on offers.
   808|- **Disputed liability stalls negotiation entirely**: In a multi-carrier case, "both sides are denying liability" — the paralegal reported to the attorney "not sure how to proceed." Disputed liability is not just a low-offer problem; it can freeze the entire negotiation process.
   809|- **PIP exhaustion monitoring is critical**: "PIP may be exhausted. Sent request for ledger" — PIP benefits running out before demand preparation creates financial urgency and affects settlement math.
   810|- **Companion batch demand is efficient**: Sending demand for companion cases simultaneously to the same adjuster — "I've attached the records/billing for [companion] as well. Please consider this our demand for policy limits" — reduces duplicate work.
   811|- **Late additional treatment delays demand**: "Sent demands on both but [client] went back for some additional treatment... should be quick to get wrapped up" — treatment that resumes after initial completion requires a second round of records collection.
   812|- **Client wanting in-person attorney meeting**: A pattern distinct from frustration — the client is "difficult. Nice guy but keeps asking to meet with you" — indicating some clients need face-to-face engagement regardless of firm communication quality.
   813|- **Standard 2-week demand follow-up cadence confirmed**: Across multiple cases, the firm follows up with adjusters approximately 2 weeks after sending demand or records to "confirm received" — this is a consistent operational pattern.
   814|- **VineSign intake to family members**: In cases where the primary client is a minor or secondary contact, intake documents may be sent "via vinesign" to a parent or family member, bypassing the client entirely.
   815|- **Same carrier for PIP and BI requires separate claim tracking**: Progressive, Elco, and other carriers handling both PIP and BI on the same case use different claim numbers and different adjusters — requiring careful tracking to avoid cross-contamination of communications.
   816|- **Coverage investigation hierarchy before decline**: Attorney directs: "check for household insurance. If not set up KAC then decline and close" — confirming the investigation order: BI > UM/UIM > household insurance > KAC > decline.
   817|

   820|- **Case decline triggers**: Two new triggers documented (client at fault, no household insurance)
   821|- **PIP exhaustion**: First documented case where PIP exhaustion is suspected
   822|- **Companion batch demand**: First documented pattern of simultaneous companion demand submission

   887|### Key New Patterns Identified
   888|- **BI-exhaustion-to-UIM transition is a standard workflow**: When BI settles at policy limits, the COOTS letter → UM claim opening → separate adjuster engagement workflow is now documented from end-to-end in one case. This should be a checklist item.
   889|- **Third-party claims administrator (TPA) demands require adjuster escalation**: When the BI claim is managed by a TPA (CCMSI, Texas Roadhouse self-insured), the demand may need to be re-sent to a different adjuster within the TPA when the first doesn't respond.
   890|- **Disputed liability creates demand-phase stalemates**: When both carriers deny fault and no arbitration has been initiated, the case sits in demand phase with the paralegal unable to proceed. This is a distinct pattern from disputed liability forcing litigation — here the case is stuck BEFORE litigation.
   891|- **Strategy-driven communication avoidance is a new unreachability pattern**: When the firm has no substantive update or strategy for a case, paralegals may avoid calling clients. This manifests as "Not sure what to tell her" and "Not sure what to tell the guy. Any ideas?" — distinct from the client being unreachable.
   892|- **Pre-existing conditions in PL cases add complexity**: A slip-and-fall where the client had prior surgery on the same body part creates a defense argument about pre-existing condition aggravation.
   893|- **Restaurant self-insured BI programs are distinct from traditional insurance**: Claims against restaurant chains may go through the restaurant's own self-insured program rather than a traditional carrier, changing adjuster dynamics.
   894|- **PIP ledger-to-provider coordination is a pre-demand step**: Providers need to know what PIP has paid so they can adjust billing before the demand amount is calculated. This step ("Ledger attached. 7k paid to [provider]. Please resubmit billing") was confirmed across multiple cases.
   895|- **Intraoperative discovery of additional injuries extends treatment**: Surgery may reveal injuries not previously diagnosed (torn rotator cuff discovered during shoulder surgery), extending the treatment timeline and delaying demand.
   896|- **Client preference for no blocked/unknown calls**: At least one client explicitly told the firm not to call from blocked numbers — confirming that client communication preferences should be captured and honored at intake.

{"mode": "patch", "patch": "*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/pip-claims-management.md
@@ evidence_count @@
-evidence_count: 27
+evidence_count: 31
@@ PIP exhaustion @@
 - PIP exhaustion was flagged as a concern in one case: "PIP may be exhausted. Sent request for ledger" — confirming that PIP ledger monitoring is critical to understand remaining benefits before demand preparation.
+- In a companion case involving 4 family members (2 adults, 2 minor children), the PIP adjuster completed the coverage investigation and communicated detailed payment decisions in a single email covering all four claimants: $4,000 reserved per person, specific billing codes denied per claimant (e.g., 97010 Hot/Cold Packs, 97014 E-stim, 97110 Therapeutic Exercise for some; 97140 Manual Therapy and 97110 for others), and ER bills excluded "per your direction." This is the most detailed PIP adjuster communication observed — showing that PIP adjusters perform individual line-item billing analysis and that the firm can direct PIP to exclude certain bill categories (such as ER bills).
+- In the same companion case, the PIP adjuster issued Reservation of Rights letters for the minor children because "proof of loss" could not be confirmed through the police report and claimant statement alone. Recorded interviews with the adult passengers were required as alternate proof of loss. This demonstrates that PIP coverage for passengers (especially minors) may require additional verification beyond the police report.
+- In a case with no PIP coverage available ("NO TREATMENT TO DATE AND NO PIP"), the BI claim proceeded independently without any PIP track. This confirms the jurisdiction-conditional nature of PIP: even in Kentucky, individual cases may lack PIP coverage if the client's own policy does not include it.
+- In a file-setup case, the firm faxed the PIP claim number to the carrier requesting acknowledgment within the first day of file setup, consistent with the standard LOR workflow. The carrier (Nationwide) was contacted via email to "forward the claim information or a copy of the denial of coverage" — confirming that claim status verification is done early.
*** Update File: /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/coverage-denial-and-litigation.md
@@ evidence_count @@
-evidence_count: 3
+evidence_count: 4
@@ The case involved multiple co-plaintiffs @@
   - As of 2025, the case remained in active litigation with depositions being scheduled — 4+ years after the accident.
+
+- In a file-setup case (auto accident, DOI 10/2025), the BI carrier (Liberty Mutual) initially acknowledged the claim with an adjuster assigned for "coverage and liability investigation." The adjuster stated: "I am working to reach my insured to verify loss details for our coverage and liability investigation." Approximately 2 weeks later, the firm's note read "NO COVERAGE ON EITHER VEHICLE." The client had no treatment to date and no PIP coverage. This represents a rapid coverage denial scenario where the entire claim collapses at file setup — unlike the stolen-vehicle case which took months of investigation. In this case, the coverage denial effectively ended the case before treatment or litigation could begin, demonstrating that coverage denial can occur at the earliest possible stage and may result in case decline rather than forced litigation.
*** Update File: /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/companion-case-management.md
@@ evidence_count @@
-evidence_count: 8
+evidence_count: 10
@@ Scheduling conflicts @@
 - Scheduling conflicts when companions need to attend the same deposition/mediation session
+
+- In a multi-passenger MVA case (DOI 11/2025), four family members (two adults, two minor children) were involved in a rear-end collision. The BI and PIP claims shared claim numbers across all companions. The PIP adjuster (at Progressive) handled all four claimants in a single email thread, completing coverage investigation and issuing individualized payment decisions per claimant. Recorded interviews with the adult passengers were required as "proof of loss" for the minors' PIP claims. This confirms that companion cases with minor children require adult passengers to provide statements as proxy evidence. The PIP adjuster denied specific billing codes differently per companion (different denied CPT codes for each child vs. each adult), demonstrating that even within the same accident, PIP payment analysis is individualized per claimant.
+- In the same case, the companion relationship was parent-children: one parent's phone number served as the contact for all four cases, and the firm used that single number for all communication. Reservation of Rights letters were issued for the minors but not the adults, creating a split coverage status across companions from the same accident.
*** Update File: /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/case-phase-transitions.md
@@ evidence_count @@
-evidence_count: 37
+evidence_count: 42
@@ split-phase statuses @@
 - In an auto accident case (DOI 10/2024, KY), the case progressed rapidly through PNC -> File Setup -> Treatment and into Negotiation phase. One BI carrier settled quickly (KFB), while a second BI carrier remained in active negotiation. This is the first observed case where one of multiple BI carriers settles while the other remains in negotiation — confirming that multi-carrier cases can have split-phase statuses.
+- In 5 additional small cases (KY MVAs, DOIs 10/2025-2/2026), the standard Lead -> File Setup transition was confirmed within 1-3 days of intake. In one case, the phase change was logged with explicit Filevine metadata: "Phase Changed By: [Staff], Old Status: Lead, New Status: File Setup." In another, the case remained in "file_setup" despite no treatment to date and no PIP — the case stalled at File Setup when coverage was denied ("NO COVERAGE ON EITHER VEHICLE"), confirming that coverage denial can freeze a case at File Setup indefinitely.
+- In a companion case with shared BI/PIP carriers, all four companions progressed through the same phase transitions in parallel, with phase changes logged within hours of each other.
*** Update File: /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/medical-records-collection.md
@@ evidence_count @@
-evidence_count: 24
+evidence_count: 27
@@ provider misidentification is a persistent risk @@
 - In Case N (premises liability estate, DOI 7/2020), records collection was complicated by provider misidentification. The firm initially requested records from "CareTenders" (a home health agency), only to discover no records existed — the actual provider was a different home health company. The surviving spouse later provided a calendar from the correct provider, enabling the correct request. Records from one diagnostic imaging provider were requested via ChartSwap, while records from the hospital required a separate LOR to the legal department before records could be released. Bills for the home health provider remained outstanding even 3+ years later during active litigation — the attorney noted during deposition prep that bills were missing. This confirms that provider misidentification is a persistent risk, especially for home health and similar ancillary providers where patients may not know the exact entity name.
+- In a treatment-phase case (DOI 1/2026), the firm sent records/bills requests via fax to PIP carrier and directly to the provider (Starlite Chiropractic) within weeks of file setup. The task "Confirm all medical providers to date including PCP" was created ~7 weeks post-DOI, confirming the periodic provider verification workflow. A separate request was faxed to the provider asking to "Please forward the records/bills" — demonstrating the direct provider request pattern alongside PIP submission.
+- In a companion case (DOI 11/2025), records requests were sent to UofL Hospital and chiropractic providers for multiple family members. The PIP adjuster's detailed billing analysis (denying specific CPT codes per patient) revealed that the PIP carrier independently reviews medical records and determines which services were "not rendered or were labeled incorrectly" — adding a new dimension to records collection where the PIP carrier's records review may conflict with the firm's records.
+- In a file-setup case (DOI 2/2026), the firm noted "unable to locate PR" (police report) and followed up with a faxed crash report request. The report was eventually received via fax. This is consistent with the pattern of police report unavailability early in the case, requiring multiple request methods (online search, then fax request).
*** Update File: /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/unreachable-client-patterns.md
@@ evidence_count @@
-evidence_count: 7
+evidence_count: 9
@@ flag for attorney review @@
 9. Consider whether unreachability is a pattern that makes the case unworkable — flag for attorney review
+10. When reaching out to a new client, provide the paralegal's direct phone number via text so the client can easily call back: "It's [name] from the firm. My direct number is [number]" — confirmed across multiple cases as an effective first-contact strategy
*** Update File: /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/police-records-open-records-requests.md
@@ evidence_count @@
-evidence_count: 3
+evidence_count: 4
@@ Even on closed/declined cases @@
 9. Even on closed/declined cases, complete pending ORRs — the records may be needed
+
+- In a file-setup case (DOI 2/2026), the police report was not immediately available — the paralegal noted "unable to locate PR." A crash report request was then faxed to the relevant jurisdiction. The report was returned via fax within weeks. This confirms the pattern that police reports may not be available through standard online channels (BuyCrash or similar) for recent accidents, requiring a direct fax request to the law enforcement agency.
*** End Patch"}

This confirms the jurisdiction-conditional nature of PIP: even in Kentucky, individual cases may lack PIP coverage if the client's own policy does not include it.
+- In a file-setup case, the firm faxed the PIP claim number to the carrier requesting acknowledgment within the first day of file setup, consistent with the standard LOR workflow. The carrier (Nationwide) was contacted via email to "forward the claim information or a copy of the denial of coverage" — confirming that claim status verification is done early.
 - In a case with an uninsured at-fault driver and no BI available, the attorney directed staff to check for household insurance before setting up KAC PIP — confirming the coverage investigation hierarchy: BI first, then UM/UIM, then household insurance, then KAC as last resort.
 - Across 17 additional small cases (all KY, DOIs 5/2025-2/2026), PIP management patterns confirmed across 11 carriers: State Farm (4 cases), Kentucky Farm Bureau (3 cases), Progressive (2 cases), National General (1), Erie Insurance (1), Travelers (1), Shelter Insurance (1), Elco Insurance (1). Key confirmations: (1) PIP LOR and ledger requests sent on day one of file setup; (2) standard request language: "please forward an updated PIP ledger along with copies of any ER related records/bills received to date"; (3) PIP ledger requests sent to carrier email/fax within the first 2 weeks; (4) in one case, the paralegal requested PIP carrier "remove any reservation for our client and process any remaining billing that is NOT ER related treatment" — confirming that PIP carriers hold reservations on benefits and the firm must direct which bills to process; (5) in a companion case pair, both companions shared the same PIP carrier and claim number (Kentucky Farm Bureau #8069631) with the same adjuster handling both; (6) Erie Insurance PIP was set up for a case with UM through Esurance — confirming that PIP and UM can be through related but distinct carrier entities; (7) one case with PIP-only (no BI identified) confirmed the coverage investigation pattern where PIP is the only available benefit.

Recorded interviews with the adult passengers were required as "proof of loss" for the minors' PIP claims. This confirms that companion cases with minor children require adult passengers to provide statements as proxy evidence. The PIP adjuster denied specific billing codes differently per companion (different denied CPT codes for each child vs. each adult), demonstrating that even within the same accident, PIP payment analysis is individualized per claimant.
+- In the same case, the companion relationship was parent-children: one parent's phone number served as the contact for all four cases, and the firm used that single number for all communication. Reservation of Rights letters were issued for the minors but not the adults, creating a split coverage status across companions from the same accident.
+
 ## Related
 
 - [[intake-and-onboarding-workflow]] — companion cases are discovered during intake

 related:
@@ -77,6 +77,8 @@
 - In an auto accident case (DOI 10/2023, KY), the case progressed PNC -> File Setup -> Treatment -> Demand in Progress -> Litigation. The presence of UM and PIP alongside BI created a three-track insurance structure. The transition to litigation occurred when BI coverage was insufficient and UM was needed. This confirms that UM/UIM coverage insufficiency is a distinct litigation trigger.
 - In an auto accident case (DOI 9/2021, KY), the case went directly from File Setup to Negotiation (negotiation phase_change logged 6/2023, ~21 months post-DOI), then to Litigation (phase_change 2/2025). The 21-month negotiation phase before litigation is the longest observed negotiation-to-litigation delay, driven by extensive treatment ($176K+ in bills) and multiple adjuster reassignments. This confirms that high-treatment-value cases with policy limits pressure generate extended negotiation before eventual litigation.
 - In an auto accident case (DOI 10/2024, KY), the case progressed rapidly through PNC -> File Setup -> Treatment and into Negotiation phase. One BI carrier settled quickly (KFB), while a second BI carrier remained in active negotiation. This is the first observed case where one of multiple BI carriers settles while the other remains in negotiation — confirming that multi-carrier cases can have split-phase statuses.
+- In 5 additional small cases (KY MVAs, DOIs 10/2025-2/2026), the standard Lead -> File Setup transition was confirmed within 1-3 days of intake. In one case, the phase change was logged with explicit Filevine metadata: "Phase Changed By: [Staff], Old Status: Lead, New Status: File Setup." In another, the case remained in "file_setup" despite no treatment to date and no PIP — the case stalled at File Setup when coverage was denied ("NO COVERAGE ON EITHER VEHICLE"), confirming that coverage denial can freeze a case at File Setup indefinitely.
+- In a companion case with shared BI/PIP carriers, all four companions progressed through the same phase transitions in parallel, with phase changes logged within hours of each other.
 - Across 17 additional small cases (all KY, DOIs 5/2025-2/2026), phase transitions followed standard patterns: PNC -> File Setup (triggered by VineSign completion), File Setup -> Treatment (triggered by insurance claims and provider setup). Cases in "demand" status (3 cases) had treatment complete and all records/bills received, confirming the treatment-complete prerequisite. Cases in "file_setup" status (5 cases) were awaiting provider setup or records requests. Cases in "treatment" status (7 cases) had active chiropractic or specialist treatment ongoing. One slip-and-fall case was categorized as "demand" despite being a premises liability case — confirming that PL cases can follow the standard demand phase when liability is clear (third-party claims administrator involved). Two companion cases from the same MVA (DOI 12/2025) showed parallel phase transitions: both moved through file setup simultaneously with shared BI and PIP carriers but independent medical treatment tracks.
 
 ## Agent Guidance

@@ -49,6 +49,9 @@
 - In an MVA case with companion plaintiffs, certified medical records were requested from a university hospital and chiropractic provider for litigation. A billing error was discovered in the chiropractic records (incorrect certified billing amount) requiring correction correspondence. This confirms that certified records must be audited — providers can certify incorrect billing amounts. Bills from EMS providers were also collected for PIP submission. The case used EvenUP for case valuation, requiring complete records before the evaluation could be generated.
 - In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff, DOI 8/2023), records were requested from 6 providers including EMS, a university hospital system (separate hospital and physician entities), chiropractic, pharmacy, and pain management. The hospital system had distinct billing entities — hospital bills were requested separately from physician bills, and each was received on different dates (spanning Nov-Dec 2024). The PIP claim was set up same day and PIP info provided to the hospital immediately. The firm also requested and received the police report from the out-of-state police department (New Albany, IN) — the report was not immediately available the day after the accident and required follow-up.
 - In Case N (premises liability estate, DOI 7/2020), records collection was complicated by provider misidentification. The firm initially requested records from "CareTenders" (a home health agency), only to discover no records existed — the actual provider was a different home health company. The surviving spouse later provided a calendar from the correct provider, enabling the correct request. Records from one diagnostic imaging provider were requested via ChartSwap, while records from the hospital required a separate LOR to the legal department before records could be released. Bills for the home health provider remained outstanding even 3+ years later during active litigation — the attorney noted during deposition prep that bills were missing. This confirms that provider misidentification is a persistent risk, especially for home health and similar ancillary providers where patients may not know the exact entity name.
+- In a treatment-phase case (DOI 1/2026), the firm sent records/bills requests via fax to PIP carrier and directly to the provider (Starlite Chiropractic) within weeks of file setup. The task "Confirm all medical providers to date including PCP" was created ~7 weeks post-DOI, confirming the periodic provider verification workflow. A separate request was faxed to the provider asking to "Please forward the records/bills" — demonstrating the direct provider request pattern alongside PIP submission.
+- In a companion case (DOI 11/2025), records requests were sent to UofL Hospital and chiropractic providers for multiple family members. The PIP adjuster's detailed billing analysis (denying specific CPT codes per patient) revealed that the PIP carrier independently reviews medical records and determines which services were "not rendered or were labeled incorrectly" — adding a new dimension to records collection where the PIP carrier's records review may conflict with the firm's records.
+- In a file-setup case (DOI 2/2026), the firm noted "unable to locate PR" (police report) and followed up with a faxed crash report request. The report was eventually received via fax. This is consistent with the pattern of police report unavailability early in the case, requiring multiple request methods (online search, then fax request).
 - Across 17 additional small cases (all KY, DOIs 5/2025-2/2026), records collection patterns confirmed existing workflow: (1) records/billing requests sent via fax and email within the first 1-2 weeks of file setup; (2) requests directed to university hospital systems (UofL Health), community hospitals, EMS providers, and chiropractic offices; (3) UofL Health records returned via ROI department with confirmation ("Received" from ROI tech); (4) hospital records and bills sometimes received on separate dates (e.g., records on day 9, bills on day 10 from same provider); (5) EMS records required separate fax request to EMS provider (Louisville Metro EMS, Anchorage EMS); (6) one case had only a single minimal-treatment provider (walk-in clinic, $99 bill) — records and bills were received together within 9 days; (7) in a slip-and-fall case managed by a third-party claims administrator, records from a chiropractic provider revealed that treatment was related to a prior MVA and not the slip-and-fall incident — creating a treatment-relatedness dispute; (8) PIP carriers (State Farm, Shelter Insurance) were sent records/billing requests in parallel with provider requests, with the standard language "please forward an updated PIP ledger along with copies of any ER related records/bills received to date"; (9) in 3 companion cases, records were collected independently for each client even when they shared the same accident and some of the same providers.
 
 ## Agent Guidance

 7. When the client does respond, immediately gather all needed information in that same conversation — you may not get another chance soon
 8. Record the client's updated phone number in the case system immediately when it changes
 9. Consider whether unreachability is a pattern that makes the case unworkable — flag for attorney review
+10. When reaching out to a new client, provide the paralegal's direct phone number via text so the client can easily call back: "It's [name] from the firm. My direct number is [number]" — confirmed across multiple cases as an effective first-contact strategy

2. `pip-claims-management` — 27->31 (added 4 new evidence items: detailed PIP adjuster line-item billing analysis, Reservation of Rights for minor passengers, no-PIP case variant, early PIP claim verification)
3. `coverage-denial-and-litigation` — 3->4 (added rapid coverage denial at file setup ending case before treatment)
4. `companion-case-management` — 8->10 (added 4-family-member PIP pattern with individualized billing code denials, minor proof-of-loss requirements)
5. `case-phase-transitions` — 37->42 (added 5 standard Lead->File Setup transitions, coverage denial freezing case at File Setup, companion parallel transitions)
6. `medical-records-collection` — 24->27 (added provider records/bills request patterns, PIP carrier independent billing review, police report fax request)
7. `unreachable-client-patterns` — 7->9 (added text-first-contact guidance with direct phone number)
8. `police-records-open-records-requests` — 3->4 (added police report unavailability with fax request pattern)

**Connection articles updated:**
1. `coverage-gaps-drive-case-decline` — 2->3 (added rapid coverage gap with no PIP fallback)
2. `coverage-denial-forces-early-litigation` — 3->4 (added alternate outcome: coverage denial + zero treatment = decline rather than litigation)

### Key New Patterns Identified
- **PIP adjuster line-item CPT code analysis per claimant**: In companion cases, PIP adjusters deny specific billing codes (97010, 97014, 97110, 97140) on a per-claimant basis, even within the same accident. This means billing disputes are individualized per companion, not per accident.
- **Reservation of Rights for minor passengers**: PIP carriers issue ROR letters for children when the police report and claimant statement don't independently verify their presence. Adult passenger recorded interviews serve as proxy proof of loss.
- **Rapid coverage denial at file setup**: Coverage denial can occur within 2 weeks of file setup ("NO COVERAGE ON EITHER VEHICLE"), effectively ending the case before any treatment or litigation begins. This is distinct from the months-long coverage investigation in the stolen-vehicle case.
- **No-PIP cases exist in Kentucky**: Even in a PIP-mandatory state, individual cases may lack PIP coverage if the client's policy doesn't include it or if no auto insurance exists.
- **Text-as-first-contact with direct number**: Paralegals sending their direct phone number via text immediately after intake ("It's [name] from the firm. My direct number is [number]") is a confirmed effective first-contact strategy.
- **Police report fax fallback**: When online police report retrieval fails ("unable to locate PR"), a faxed crash report request to the jurisdiction is the standard fallback, with turnaround in weeks.
- **Cases can stall at onboarding with zero activity**: One case had a BI claim number but zero activity logs — indicating that some cases are opened in the system but never actively worked. This may represent dropped leads or abandoned intake.
- **ER bill exclusion by firm direction**: The firm can direct PIP carriers to exclude ER-related bills from PIP processing, reserving PIP benefits for other providers. This is a strategic PIP management decision.

### Index Rebuilt
The wiki/index.md was completely rebuilt from all 48 concept articles and 16 connection articles currently on disk. The index now reflects the true current state of all articles with accurate phase assignments, categories, confidence levels, and evidence counts.

- **Medium confidence articles**: 18
- **Low confidence articles**: 28
- **Total cases compiled across all batches**: 49+

### Remaining Gaps (Final Assessment)
- Settlement disbursement: Still no cases with completed final disbursement
- Counter-offer exchange documentation: Detailed back-and-forth negotiation tactics
- VA lien negotiation/reduction outcomes
- KAC process resolution
- UIM settlement outcomes
- Dog bite/dental injury case values
- Workers' compensation case resolution
- Medicare lien reduction via BCRC
- Government entity defendant case resolution
- Employer WC obstruction resolution"}

{"output": "concepts/coverage-denial-and-litigation.md:64:- In a file-setup case (auto accident, DOI 10/2025), the BI carrier (Liberty Mutual) initially acknowledged the claim with an adjuster assigned for "coverage and liability investigation." The adjuster stated: "I am working to reach my insured to verify loss details for our coverage and liability investigation." Approximately 2 weeks later, the firm's note read "NO COVERAGE ON EITHER VEHICLE." The client had no treatment to date and no PIP coverage. This represents a rapid coverage denial scenario where the entire claim collapses at file setup — unlike the stolen-vehicle case which took months of investigation. In this case, the coverage denial effectively ended the case before treatment or litigation could begin, demonstrating that coverage denial can occur at the earliest possible stage and may result in case decline rather than forced litigation.
concepts/medical-records-collection.md:52:- In a treatment-phase case (DOI 1/2026), the firm sent records/bills requests via fax to PIP carrier and directly to the provider (Starlite Chiropractic) within weeks of file setup. The task "Confirm all medical providers to date including PCP" was created ~7 weeks post-DOI, confirming the periodic provider verification workflow. A separate request was faxed to the provider asking to "Please forward the records/bills" — demonstrating the direct provider request pattern alongside PIP submission.
concepts/medical-records-collection.md:53:- In a companion case (DOI 11/2025), records requests were sent to UofL Hospital and chiropractic providers for multiple family members. The PIP adjuster's detailed billing analysis (denying specific CPT codes per patient) revealed that the PIP carrier independently reviews medical records and determines which services were "not rendered or were labeled incorrectly" — adding a new dimension to records collection where the PIP carrier's records review may conflict with the firm's records.
concepts/medical-records-collection.md:54:- In a file-setup case (DOI 2/2026), the firm noted "unable to locate PR" (police report) and followed up with a faxed crash report request. The report was eventually received via fax. This is consistent with the pattern of police report unavailability early in the case, requiring multiple request methods (online search, then fax request).
concepts/client-frustration-escalation.md:85:- In a recent MVA case (DOI 2/2026, treatment/demand phase), the client left a voicemail: "I have been trying to call you and get in contact with you or somebody and I can not get through. If you could call me when you get a chance... I would greatly appreciate it." The voicemail transcription showed the client attempted to reach the firm by calling both the main number and the managing attorney directly. This occurred less than 2 months after intake, demonstrating that client frustration can begin very early in the case lifecycle — not just during the demand/negotiation phase.
concepts/pip-claims-management.md:59:- In one case (DOI 2/2026), the firm noted "NO PIP COVERAGE" at intake on the same day. The case proceeded with BI only (Travelers Insurance). This confirms that not all KY auto accident cases have PIP, and the coverage determination must be made immediately at intake. This is the second observed no-PIP case in the wiki.
concepts/police-records-open-records-requests.md:78:- In a file-setup case (DOI 2/2026), the police report was not immediately available — the paralegal noted "unable to locate PR." A crash report request was then faxed to the relevant jurisdiction. The report was returned via fax within weeks. This confirms the pattern that police reports may not be available through standard online channels (BuyCrash or similar) for recent accidents, requiring a direct fax request to the law enforcement agency.
concepts/companion-case-management.md:65:- In a multi-passenger MVA case (DOI 11/2025), four family members (two adults, two minor children) were involved in a rear-end collision. The BI and PIP claims shared claim numbers across all companions. The PIP adjuster (at Progressive) handled all four claimants in a single email thread, completing coverage investigation and issuing individualized payment decisions per claimant. Recorded interviews with the adult passengers were required as "proof of loss" for the minors' PIP claims. This confirms that companion cases with minor children require adult passengers to provide statements as proxy evidence. The PIP adjuster denied specific billing codes differently per companion (different denied CPT codes for each child vs. each adult), demonstrating that even within the same accident, PIP payment analysis is individualized per claimant.
concepts/companion-case-management.md:67:- In an MVA companion pair (DOI 11/2025), a father (Sr) and son were injured in the same accident. Both cases shared identical BI and PIP claim numbers with the same State Farm adjusters. Both were treated at the same hospital ER and referred to the same chiropractic provider. Records and bills were collected independently per companion, and both reached "all records received" status around the same time. Both cases entered the demand phase simultaneously, confirming that companion cases with aligned treatment timelines should target synchronized demand preparation. The son's case had bills requested and received on the same day (same-day turnaround from the chiropractor), suggesting that when one companion's records are ready, the other's may be as well from shared providers.
concepts/treatment-gap-defense.md:38:- In a slip-and-fall case (DOI 1/2026), a chiropractic provider reported that "all the treatment client had there was related to a previous MVA and not the slip and fall" — creating a treatment-relatedness dispute rather than a treatment gap per se. The client had concurrent treatment at the same provider for two separate incidents, and the provider's own records attributed all treatment to the earlier MVA. This is a variant of the unrelated-treatment-attribution defense where the treating provider itself confirms the attribution, making the adjuster's position significantly stronger than when the adjuster unilaterally attributes treatment to a different cause.
